{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4646","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4646/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4646/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4646/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4646","id":2464927137,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6S682h","number":4646,"title":"Option needed to get standard compliance; unclear documentation","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-08-14T05:23:18Z","updated_at":"2024-08-14T21:43:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This program gives standard-conforming output with lfortran if and only if the option ```--print-leading-space``` is used.
```
program space1
  write(*,*) 'Hello world!'
  write(*,"(A)") 'Hello again!'
end program space1
```
I am one of the users who prefers standard-conforming behaviour without compile-time options, rather than needing them to get it in some circumstances. #2672 recommended the Python system (does it omit the leading space?)  but Fortran programs should follow the Fortran standard. With format ```*``` it requires the leading space and gives the compiler a lot of choice after that.
 
The lfortran documentation says
```
--print-leading-space: Print leading white space if format is unspecified
```
That is unclear because (a) PRINT and WRITE are different statements and (b) there are three ways to use a WRITE statement:
unformatted, or with format ```*```, or with an explicit format e.g. "(A)".
PRINT does not allow the unformatted case. I had thought "format is unspecified" meant the unformatted case for a WRITE statement because the other two cases both specify a format. I therefore suggest that the documentation be amended to
```
--print-leading-space: Print leading white space if the format is *
```
But my preference would be to make ```--print-leading-space``` the default and have a new option
```
--no-leading-space: Omit the leading space when writing with format *
```

```
program isotest ! the 8 numbers already in iso_fortran_env in f2003  
  use,intrinsic:: iso_fortran_env, only: input_unit,output_unit,error_unit,&
       iostat_end,iostat_eor, &
       numeric_storage_size,character_storage_size,file_storage_size
  print *,'ISO_FORTRAN_ENV module constants:'
  print "(1X,A,1X,I0,A)",'input_unit ',input_unit,'',&
       'output_unit',output_unit,'', 'error_unit ',error_unit,'',&
       'iostat_end',iostat_end,'', 'iostat_eor',iostat_eor,'',&
       'file_storage_size     ',file_storage_size,' bits',&
       'numeric_storage_size ',numeric_storage_size,' bits',&
       'character_storage_size',character_storage_size,' bits'
end program isotest
```

```

But without ```--std=f23``` lfortran 0.39.0 does what I think is right.
```
! whatsize.f90 based on @zaikunzhang's in Fortran Discourse 12 Apr 2024)
program whatsize
  implicit none
  integer, parameter :: k = 2000 ! original had 10000
  integer :: i,nsize
  real :: y(k) = 1.0

  do i = 1000, k, 500
     nsize = size(outprod(y(1:i), y(1:i)))
     print "(2(1X,I0))", i,nsize
  end do

contains
  function outprod(y, z) result(x)
    implicit none
    real, intent(in) :: y(:), z(:)
    real :: x(size(y), size(z))
    integer :: i, j
    
    do i = 1, size(y)
       do j = 1, size(z)
          x(i, j) = y(i) * z(j)
       end do
    end do
  end function outprod
end program whatsize
```

...
   |
25 |      end function outprod
   | ...^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 
```

  character fmt*10, line*80, letters*26
  integer i
  letters = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'

  print "(A)", trim(line)
end program fmtbuga
```

With ```--std=f23``` only the one calling ```system``` runs; without it, only the other one runs. I would have expected ```system``` to work (if at all) only without ```--std=f23```, and ```execute_command_line``` to work in both cases. The programs and their outputs from lfortran 0.39.0 in a Linux machine:

Calling ```system```
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ cat systema.f90
program systema
  call system('echo Hello world')
end program systema
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran --std=f23 systema.f90
Hello world
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran  systema.f90
semantic error: Function 'system' not found or not implemented yet (if it is intrinsic)
 --> systema.f90:2:3
  |
2 |   call system('echo Hello world')
  |   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
```

Calling ```execute_command_line```
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ cat systemb.f90
program systemb
  call execute_command_line('echo Hello world')
end program systemb
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran --std=f23 systemb.f90
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran  systemb.f90
Hello world
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4676/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4676/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

0.40.0 in my system (now Ubuntu 24.04) failed to insert the blank space between 32 and 2147483647 which was required for * (list-directed) output between two integers , which were an array section, in my program arraysectiond.f90. The output was on two lines but should have been on one. Lfortran 0.36.0 was correct in both respects (see #4501, which also reported another problem). The program:
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ cat arraysectiond.f90
  implicit none
  integer,parameter:: k = selected_int_kind(9) ! default integer
  integer :: iarray(3,4)
  iarray(1,1:2) = [bit_size(1_k),huge(1_k)]
  print *,kind(1_k),iarray(1,1:2)
end program
```
Output from lfortran 0.40.0 (wrong) :
```
4
322147483647
```
Output from lfortran 0.36.0 (right) :
```
4 32 2147483647
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4705/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4705/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

```
  Binary file "/home/john/conda_root/envs/lf/bin/lfortran", local address: 0x7a7088
  Binary file "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6", local address: 0x29e3f

```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4501","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4501/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4501/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4501/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4501","id":2406728379,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6Pc8K7","number":4501,"title":"segmentation fault for array section of non-default integer kind","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-07-13T06:13:24Z","updated_at":"2024-07-13T06:13:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The only difference between these two programs is in their lines defining the integer k, which is used as the kind of 1_k. The first program segfaults and the second runs happily. Why?
Evidence from lfortran 0.36.0 in an x86_64 Ubuntu 22.04 system when k is 1:
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ cat arraysectionc.f90
  implicit none
  integer,parameter:: k = selected_int_kind(0) ! smallest integer type
  integer :: iarray(3,4)
  iarray(1,1:2) = [bit_size(1_k),huge(1_k)]
  print *,kind(1_k),iarray(1,1:2)
end program
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran arraysectionc.f90
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
(lf) john:~/Test$ 
```
But when k is 4:
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ cat arraysectiond.f90
  implicit none
  integer,parameter:: k = selected_int_kind(9) ! default integer
  integer :: iarray(3,4)
  iarray(1,1:2) = [bit_size(1_k),huge(1_k)]
  print *,kind(1_k),iarray(1,1:2)
end program
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran arraysectiond.f90
4 32 2147483647 
(lf) john:~/Test$","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4501/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4501/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4527","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4527/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4527/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4527/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4527","id":2417438619,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6QFy-b","number":4527,"title":"READ without a unit not allowed","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-07-18T21:18:42Z","updated_at":"2024-07-18T21:18:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In this program lfortran disallows the READ statement, which was valid Fortran 77 and is still valid Fortran 2023 (12.6.1 allows it without a unit, and 12.6.4.3 says where the reading is from). The program:
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ cat readtest1.f90
implicit none
  character reply
  print *,'Enter one character.'
  READ '(A)', REPLY
  print *,'You said ',reply 
end program
```
AMD flang, gfortran, g95, ifort and ifx all compiled and ran it but this is what lfortran 0.36.0 did in a Ubuntu 22.04 x86_64 system:
```
lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran readtest1.f90
syntax error: Token ''(A)'' (of type 'string') is unexpected here
 --> readtest1.f90:4:8
  |
4 |   READ '(A)', REPLY
  |        ^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
(lf) john:~/Test$
```


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
(lf) john:~/Test$","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4527/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4527/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4530","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4530/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4530/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4530/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4530","id":2420586269,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6QRzcd","number":4530,"title":"ESw.dEe format misinterpreted when e is too small","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-07-20T05:33:26Z","updated_at":"2024-07-20T05:33:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This program uses ```ESw.dEe``` format with ```w``` sometimes too small and ```e``` always too small to write the value ```3e20```:
```
  implicit none
  integer w,io
  character(12) fmt
  do w = 6,9
     write(fmt,'(A,I0,A)') '(A,ES',w,'.2E1)'
     write(*,fmt,iostat=io) fmt//' ',3e20
     if(io/=0) cycle
  end do
end program
```
Lfortran gives what I think is the wrong output (and so does AMD flang):
```
(A,ES6.2E1)  ******
(A,ES7.2E1)  *******
(A,ES8.2E1)  3.00E+20
(A,ES9.2E1)   3.00E+20
```
Gfortran, ifort and ifx all give what I think is the right output:
```
(A,ES6.2E1)  ******
(A,ES7.2E1)  *******
(A,ES8.2E1)  ********
(A,ES9.2E1)  *********
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4530/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4530/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Both gfortran and lfortran do detect it at run time. As compilers are free to choose their own format for 
 ```print *, log_gamma(0.0)``` I used an explicit format, hoping for the output ```Inf``` with an upper case ```I``` required by the F2023 standard. This is what I got. Lfortran seems to have a bug with format ```"(F0.0)"```
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ cat badlog_gamma.F90
program main
  implicit none
  real x
  x = 0.0
  print "(F0.0)", log_gamma(x)
end program main
(lf) john:~/Test$ gfortran-13 badlog_gamma.F90 && ./a.out
Inf
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran badlog_gamma.F90
-9223372036854775807.f
(lf) john:~/Test$ 
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2254677694/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

It compiles and runs OK with gfortran and ifort. Lfortran gives an error message that would have been correct in Fortran 2003 but is wrong in Fortran 2008 or later because the F2008 standard 6.1 says a variable is a designator, which may be a complex-part-designator, and 6.4.4 says that that is designator%RE or designator%IM. The same wording is in F2023 9.1 and 9.4.4.
```
program main
  complex :: x(3)

  x(1)%re = 1
  x(2)%re = 2
  x(3)%re = 3
  
  x(1)%im = 4
  x(2)%im = 5
  x(3)%im = 6
  
  write(*,*) x
  
  call foo(x%re)
  call foo(x%im)
  contains
  
  subroutine foo(x)
    real, intent(in) :: x(3)
    
    write(*,*) "x", x
  end subroutine
end program
```
The lfortran output:
```
semantic error: The LHS of assignment can only be a variable or an array reference
 --> badcomplex.F90:4:3
  |
4 |   x(1)%re = 1
  |   ^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4553/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4553/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

```

1/zerosp = "           inf "

It reveals another problem: only two values printed instead of four, both wrong, and the second one > huge(1) and different each time.
```
program subproblem
  implicit none
  integer,parameter:: k(5) = [-1,2,3,3,3], ksize = size(k)
  integer:: n
  integer,parameter:: diffs1(ksize-1) = [(abs(k(n)-k(n-1)), n=2,ksize)]
  print "(A,4(1X,I0),A)", 'diffs1 in main line  = "',diffs1,'"'
  call subprogram
contains
  subroutine subprogram
    integer i
    print "(A,4(1X,I0),A)",'diffs1 in subprogram = "',&
         (diffs1(i),i=1,ksize-1),'"'
  end subroutine subprogram
end program subproblem
```
Outputs from two runs with lfortran:
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran subproblemb.f90
diffs1 in main line  = " 3 1 0 0"
diffs1 in subprogram = " 16 94802073821304 
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran subproblemb.f90
diffs1 in main line  = " 3 1 0 0"
diffs1 in subprogram = " 16 94187703464056 
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2259640155/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

(lf2) john:~/Test/Lfortran$ cat eptest.f90
program eptest
  implicit none
  integer,parameter::dp=kind(1d0),nextp=selected_real_kind(precision(1d0)+1)
  integer,parameter::ep = merge(nextp,dp,nextp>0)
  print *,'pi=acos(-1.0_dp)=',acos(-1.0_dp)
  print *,'pi=acos(-1.0_ep)=',acos(-1.0_ep)
end program eptest
(lf2) john:~/Test/Lfortran$ lfortran eptest.f90
semantic error: Initialization of `ep` must reduce to a compile time constant.
 --> eptest.f90:4:3
  |

  |   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

I gave up trying to build from source tarball and used 
```
conda install lfortran -c conda-forge
```
instead. That worked but I can compile and run a program if and only if I don't use ```--version```. Other compilers let me use their equivalent of ```--version``` while compiling a program.   Evidence:
```
(lf) john:~$ cat hello.f
      print *,'Hello World'
      end program
(lf) john:~$ lfortran --version hello.f
LFortran version: 0.36.0
Platform: Linux
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
(lf) john:~$ lfortran hello.f
Hello World
(lf) john:~$ 
```

It prints one integer for each instead of the expected two, the values it prints are equal, and if lfortran 0.36.0 is used again quite different values are printed, as  is shown below. The program ```nandp.f90``` is:
```
   use ieee_arithmetic
  implicit none
  double precision dpnan(2)
  dpnan = ieee_value(dpnan, [ieee_signaling_nan,ieee_quiet_nan] )
  print "(A,2(1X,I0))",'IEEE signaling NaN =', transfer(dpnan(1),[1])
  print "(A,2(1X,I0))",'IEEE quiet     NaN =', transfer(dpnan(2),[1])
end program
```
The output from compiling and running it 3 times:
```
lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran nandp.f90
IEEE signaling NaN = 4201219760 
IEEE quiet     NaN = 4201219760 
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran nandp.f90
IEEE signaling NaN = 2383303968 
IEEE quiet     NaN = 2383303968 
(lf) john:~/Test$ lfortran nandp.f90
IEEE signaling NaN = 1308315952 
IEEE quiet     NaN = 1308315952 
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4258/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4258/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

```
(lf) john:~/Test$ cat naninfdp.f90
  use ieee_arithmetic
  implicit none
  double precision xdp
  print *,'IEEE signaling NaN =',ieee_value(xdp,ieee_signaling_nan)
  print *,'IEEE quiet     NaN =',ieee_value(xdp,ieee_quiet_nan)
  print *,'IEEE positive  Inf =',ieee_value(xdp,ieee_positive_inf)
  print *,'IEEE negative  Inf =',ieee_value(xdp,ieee_negative_inf)
end program
```

```

IEEE negative  Inf = 6.95288454156656424e-310

IEEE quiet     NaN = 6.95325317017252505e-310

IEEE negative  Inf = 6.95325317017252505e-310
(lf) john:~/Test$

```
program readmsg
  implicit none ! try both iostat and iomsg in read statement
  integer n,ios
  character(200):: msg,fmt = "(A,I0,1X,A,'""',A,'""')"
  print *,'Enter an integer'
  read(*,*,iostat=ios,iomsg=msg) n
  if(ios>0) print fmt,'Error reading integer; ios=',ios,'msg=',trim(msg)
  if(ios<0) print fmt,'Unexpected end of input; ios=',ios,'msg=',trim(msg)
  print *,'You said',n
end program readmsg
```
Results from 3 runs of that program: the first should have been error-free, the others should have printed non-empty values of ```msg```. 
```
(lf) john:~/Test$ lf readmsg.f90
Enter an integer
666
Error reading integer; ios=1096621737 msg=""
You said 666
(lf) john:~/Test$ lf readmsg.f90
Enter an integer
2222222222222222222222222222222222222
Error reading integer; ios=469445769 msg=""
You said -1
(lf) john:~/Test$ lf readmsg.f90
Enter an integer
x
Unexpected end of input; ios=-1282212455 msg=""
You said 32766
(lf) john:~/Test$
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2187585848/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

arperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-04T03:12:31Z","updated_at":"2024-05-04T03:12:31Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Having found that lfortran would not allow T in a format string (valid Fortran ever since f77) I wrote this little program to check my workaround, which 5 other compilers all allowed. But if I tried compiling with the ```-g --debug-with-line-column```  options as recommended when I just tried ```-g```, I got an ICE as I did in #3900, although this time no allocatable scalar was involved. I quote the Fortran program and its outputs with various options. The output was useful only with no options at all specified!
```
(lf2) john:~/Test$ cat fmtbug.f90
program fmtbug
  character fmt*26, line*80
  fmt =  '(1X,A,T33,A4,6X,99(A5,6X))'
  write(line,fmt) 'a','b','c','d','e'
  print "(A)", trim(line)
  write(line,"(1X,A,A4,6X,99(A5,6X))") 'a'//repeat(' ',30), 'b','c','d','e'
  print "(A)", trim(line)
end program fmtbug
(lf2) john:~/Test$ lfortran fmtbug.f90
Unsupported or unrecognized `T` in format string
(lf2) john:~/Test$ lfortran -g  fmtbug.f90
semantic error: The `emit_debug_line_column` is not enabled; please use the `--debug-with-line-column` option to get the correct location information


Note: Please report unclear or confusing messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
(lf2) john:~/Test$ lfortran -g --debug-with-line-column fmtbug.f90
Internal Compiler Error: Unhandled exception
Traceback (most recent call last):
  Binary file "/home/john/conda_root/envs/lf2/bin/lfortran", local address: 0x726858

(lf2) john:~/Test$ cat fmtbuga.f90
program fmtbuga
  character fmt*10, line*80, letters*26
  integer i
  letters = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
  fmt = '(99(1X,A))'
  write(line,fmt) (letters(i:i), i=1,26)
  print "(A)", trim(line)
end program fmtbuga
(lf2) john:~/Test$ lf fmtbuga.f90
code generation error: Printing support is not available for `character[:]` type.
 --> fmtbuga.f90:6:14
  |
6 |   write(line,fmt) (letters(i:i), i=1,26)
  |              ^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear or confusing messages as bugs at

","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-05-04T08:07:23Z","updated_at":"2024-05-04T08:07:23Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In an attempt to work around the problem reported in #3960 I tried to convert a string of 26 characters into an array of 26 single characters but ```transfer``` seems to have converted it into an array of 64 elements. It compiled and ran with other compilers but
this is what lfortran (aliased to ```lf``` in my system) did with it.
```
(lf2) john:~/Test$ cat fmtbugb.f90
program fmtbugb
  character(1) fmt*10, line*80, lstring*26, larray(26)
  integer i

```
(lf2) john:~/Test$ cat bitsizetest.f90
program bitsizetest
  integer,parameter:: ik1 = selected_int_kind(1)
  print "(A,I0)", &
       'ik1                   = ',ik1,&
       'kind(bit_size(1_ik1)) = ',kind(bit_size(1_ik1)), &
       'kind(huge(1_ik1))     = ',kind(huge(1_ik1)),&
       'huge(1_ik1)           = ',huge(1_ik1)
end program bitsizetest
(lf2) john:~/Test$ lfortran bitsizetest.f90
ik1                   = 1
kind(bit_size(1_ik1)) = 4
kind(huge(1_ik1))     = 1
huge(1_ik1)           = 127
(lf2) john:~/Test$ 
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3999/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3999/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

function"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-05-24T05:28:15Z","updated_at":"2024-05-24T23:53:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In the `src/libasr/intrinsic_func_registry_util_gen.py` file,  I see one of the functions named `fix`. 
```python
    "Fix": [
        {
            "args": [("real",)],
            "ret_type_arg_idx": 0
        }
    ],
```
```fortran
program main
    print*, fix(1.5)
end
```
```console
$ lfortran a.f90 && ./a.out
1.00000000e+00
1.00000000e+00
$ gfortran a.f90 && ./a.out
/usr/bin/ld: /tmp/ccObtuGz.o: in function `MAIN__':
a.f90:(.text+0x51): undefined reference to `fix_'
collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
```

Does this function really exists? 
If yes, we need to add test for intrinsic `fix`.
If no, should we remove it?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4080/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4080/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2130526044","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4080#issuecomment-2130526044","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4080","id":2130526044,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc5-_T9c","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-24T23:53:33Z","updated_at":"2024-05-24T23:53:33Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Neither ```fix``` nor ```trunc``` is a Fortran 2023 intrinsic function. The ```fix``` function is superfluous in Fortran. If ```fix(x)``` truncates towards zero and its value is of the same real type as ```x``` then ```aint(x)``` does exactly that job. If it converts to integer type, Fortran has ```int(aint(x))```, or for those who want the "belt and braces" approach, ```nint(aint(x))```. All those functions allow a second optional argument to change from one real or integer kind to another. @HarshitaKalani has not explained the difference between ```trunc``` and ```fix```. Is ```trunc(x)``` the same as Fortran's ```anint(x)```? If not, what is it?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2131450040/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'm not sure if an implied-do is supposed to have a different scope for the variable.  Specifically this program:

```Fortran
program scope

    implicit none
    integer :: i
    real :: a(10)

    i = 999
    a = [((2.5*i), i=1,10)]

    print *, i

end program scope
```

...prints "999" with gfortran and ifx, but "10" with LFortran.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4099/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4099/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2134108529","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4099#issuecomment-2134108529","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4099","id":2134108529,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc5_M-lx","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-27T22:32:53Z","updated_at":"2024-05-27T22:32:53Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"F2023 section 19.4 is not easy to follow. Metcalf et al. "Modern Fortran Explained" (2023 edition p.138) says 
```
The scope of the variable is the constructor-implied-do. Other statements, or even other
parts of the array constructor, may refer to another variable having the same name. The
value of the other variable is unaffected by execution of the array constructor and is available
outside the constructor-implied-do.
```
Exactly the same wording is in Metcalf et al. "Fortran 95/2003 Explained" (2004 edition p.122). That makes me think that  gfortran and ifx are right on this matter and lfortran has a bug.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2134108529/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3789","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3789/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3789/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3789/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3789","id":2227187869,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6EwDCd","number":3789,"title":"ieee values wrong?","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-04-05T06:39:39Z","updated_at":"2024-04-05T06:39:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The program below prints different results from gfortran and ifx, which agree with each other. It tests what  5 different logical functions of 4  different IEEE values give. Only the double precision version is given here. Single precision behaved similarly. My first version would not compile with lfotrtran because it had : in a format.The program and its lfortran output are:
```
! Testing Inf and NaN 
program infnan
  use,intrinsic:: ieee_arithmetic
  implicit none
  integer     ,parameter:: dp = kind(1d0)
  character(*),parameter:: fmt = "(A,4L2)" ! lfortran can't use (A,:,4L2)
  real(dp):: xdp(4)
  xdp = [ ieee_value(1d0, ieee_positive_inf),&
       ieee_value(1d0, ieee_negative_inf),&
       ieee_value(1d0, ieee_quiet_nan),&
       ieee_value(1d0, ieee_signaling_nan) ]

  print fmt,repeat(' ',16)//'Inf NaN'
  print fmt,repeat(' ',16)//'+ - Q S' 
  print fmt,'isinfdp(xdp)   ',isinfdp(xdp)
  print fmt,'isposinfdp(xdp)',isposinfdp(xdp)
  print fmt,'isneginfdp(xdp)',isneginfdp(xdp)
  print fmt,'isnandp(xdp)   ',isnandp(xdp)
  print fmt,'isfinitedp(xdp)',isfinitedp(xdp)
contains

  elemental logical function isnandp(x)
    real(dp),intent(in)::            x
    isnandp = (x/=x)
  end function isnandp

  elemental logical function isinfdp(x)
    real(dp),intent(in)::            x
    isinfdp = (abs(x)>huge(x))
  end function isinfdp

  elemental logical function isposinfdp(x)
    real(dp),intent(in)::            x
    isposinfdp = (x>huge(x))
  end function isposinfdp

  elemental logical function isneginfdp(x)
    real(dp),intent(in)::            x
    isneginfdp = (x<-huge(x))
  end function isneginfdp

  elemental logical function isfinitedp(x)
    real(dp),intent(in)::            x
    isfinitedp = (abs(x)<=huge(x))
  end function isfinitedp

end program infnan
 
(lf2) john:~/Test$ lfortran infnan.f90
                Inf NaN
                + - Q S
isinfdp(xdp)    F F F F
isposinfdp(xdp) F F F F
isneginfdp(xdp) F F F F
isnandp(xdp)    F F F F
isfinitedp(xdp) T T T T
```
Output from that program using gfortran or ifx:
```
                 Inf NaN
                + - Q S
isinfdp(xdp)    T T F F
isposinfdp(xdp) T F F F
isneginfdp(xdp) F T F F
isnandp(xdp)    F F T T
isfinitedp(xdp) F F F F
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484","id":1651669466,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc5icnna","number":1484,"title":"Not all constant expressions are accepted as kind parameters","user":{"login":"fxcoudert","id":1980544,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5ODA1NDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1980544?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert","html_url":"https://github.com/fxcoudert","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-04-03T09:15:39Z","updated_at":"2024-04-06T07:42:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There are issues where valid constant expressions are not accepted as kind parameters (they should be):

```
program test
  integer(kind=int(4.1)**2/4) :: x
  print *, huge(x)
end
```

gives

```
semantic error: Only Integer literals or expressions which reduce to constant Integer are accepted as kind parameters.
```

Also rejected (while it shouldn't):

```
  integer(kind=transfer(4_1, 0)) :: x1
  integer(kind=kind(0)) :: x2
  integer(kind=(4+0)) :: x3

  integer :: i
  integer(kind=sum([(i, i=0,3)])-2) :: x4
  integer(kind=INT(61*BESSEL_JN(4, 2.5))) :: x5
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2041005557","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484#issuecomment-2041005557","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1484","id":2041005557,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc55p0X1","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-06T07:42:03Z","updated_at":"2024-04-06T07:42:03Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"lfortran does not accept merge in a constant expression (valid in f2008 but not f2003):

Gfortran is happy with the program, ifort and ifx complain because they write xstring as
a value > huge(1d0). 
```
(lf2) john:~/Test/Lfortran$ cat startest.f90
program startest
  double precision:: x = huge(1d0)
  character(40) xstring
  write(xstring,*) x
  read (xstring,*) x
  write(*,*) x
end program startest
(lf2) john:~/Test/Lfortran$ lfortran startest.f90
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@startest.x = internal global double 0.000000e+00
@0 = private unnamed_addr constant [2 x i8] c" \00", align 1
@1 = private unnamed_addr constant [2 x i8] c"\0A\00", align 1
@2 = private unnamed_addr constant [8 x i8] c"%23.17e\00", align 1
@3 = private unnamed_addr constant [3 x i8] c"%d\00", align 1
@4 = private unnamed_addr constant [2 x i8] c" \00", align 1
@5 = private unnamed_addr constant [2 x i8] c"\0A\00", align 1
@6 = private unnamed_addr constant [10 x i8] c"%23.17e%s\00", align 1

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  call void @_lpython_set_argv(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  store double 0x7FEFFFFFFFFFFFFF, double* @startest.x, align 8
  %xstring = alloca i8*, align 8
  %2 = call i8* (i32, ...) @_lfortran_malloc(i32 41)
  call void (i32, i8*, ...) @_lfortran_string_init(i32 41, i8* %2)
  store i8* %2, i8** %xstring, align 8
  %3 = load i8*, i8** %xstring, align 8
  %4 = alloca i32, align 4
  %5 = load double, double* @startest.x, align 8
  call void (i8**, i32*, i8*, ...) @_lfortran_string_write(i8** %xstring, i32* %4, i8* getelementptr inbounds ([8 x i8], [8 x i8]* @2, i32 0, i32 0), double %5)

It fails to do so, and stops with a segmentation fault after (or when?) ```test``` is called for the second time. The subroutine ```test``` allocates an internal variable ```arr``` which ought to be deallocated automatically on exiting ```test```. Is the bug there, or is it because ```source=``` is used in the allocation?
``(lf2) john:~/Test/Lfortran$ cat allocatesource.f90
! allocatesource.f90 ! allocate with source=- is a F2008 feature
program allocatesource
  call test( n= 100 )
  print "(A,2I12)",'expected   result',100,100
  call test( n= 200 )
  print "(A,2I12)",'expected   result',200,200
contains
  subroutine test( n )
    integer, intent(in) :: n
    integer, allocatable :: arr(:)
    allocate( arr( 2 ), source= n )
    print "(A,2I12)",'subroutine result',arr
    !! explicit deallocation not needed unless arr saved
  end subroutine test
end program allocatesource
  
(lf2) john:~/Test/Lfortran$ lfortran allocatesource.f90
subroutine result

expected   result         100         100
subroutine result
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
(lf2) john:~/Test/Lfortran$ 
```
Four other compilers (gfortran, ifort, ifx, AOCC flang) all give this output, which I think is correct:
```
subroutine result         100         100
expected   result         100         100
subroutine result         200         200
expected   result         200         200
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3819/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3819/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

    print *, arr

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3906","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3906/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3906/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3906/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3906","id":2252450328,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6GQaoY","number":3906,"title":"x = huge(x) disallowed in a constant expression","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-04-19T08:53:44Z","updated_at":"2024-04-19T08:53:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The declaration of x in this program appears to be standard-conforming, by F2018 10.1.12 para 2. Five compilers including g95 allowed it, but lfortran did not:
```
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ cat bigdeclare.f90
program bigdeclare
  implicit none
  real(kind(1d0)):: x = huge(x)
  print *,x
end program bigdeclare
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ lfortran bigdeclare.f90
semantic error: Variable 'x' is not declared
 --> bigdeclare.f90:3:30
  |
3 |   real(kind(1d0)):: x = huge(x)
  |                              ^ 'x' is undeclared


Note: Please report unclear or confusing messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ 
```
That paragraph in the F2018 standard says
"If a constant expression includes a specification inquiry that depends on a type parameter or an array bound of an entity specified in the same specification-part, the type parameter or array bound shall be specified in a prior specification of the specification-part. The prior specification may be to the left of the specification inquiry in the same statement, but shall not be within the same entity-decl unless the specification inquiry appears within an initialization."

Here the prior specification is the first x, and huge(x) is a specification inquiry.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3906/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3906/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

```
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ cat allocinmodule.f90 
! allocinmodule.f90
module test_m ! allocate with source=- is a F2008 feature
    implicit none
contains
subroutine test( n )
    integer, intent(in) :: n
    integer, allocatable :: arr(:)

    allocate( arr( 2 ), source= n )
    print *, arr
    !! No explicit deallocation here.
end
end module

program allocinmodule
  use test_m
  call test( n= 100 )
  call test( n= 200 )
end program allocinmodule

1680326320 5 
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ 
```

The first time it should give cmdstat the value 0 and print the user's name and nothing else.
The second time it should give cmdstat a positive value and print cmdmsg.

The program and its lfortran and ifx outputs:
```
program executecommands4
  implicit none
  call executemycommand('whoami')
  call executemycommand('FOOBAR')
contains
  subroutine executemycommand(cmd)
    character(*),intent(in):: cmd
    integer cs
    character(200):: cmsg
    call execute_command_line(trim(cmd),cmdstat=cs,cmdmsg=cmsg)
    if(cs/=0) write(*,"(A,I0)") '*** After execute_command_line('//&
         trim(cmd)//'), cmdstat=',cs,'*** Message was '//trim(cmsg)
end subroutine executemycommand
end program executecommands4
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ lfortran executecommands4.f90 
john
*** After execute_command_line(whoami), cmdstat=1
*** Message was 
sh: 1: FOOBAR: not found
*** After execute_command_line(FOOBAR), cmdstat=1
*** Message was 
```
The ifx output:
```
john
sh: 1: FOOBAR: not found
*** After execute_command_line(FOOBAR), cmdstat=124
*** Message was Invalid command supplied to EXECUTE_COMMAND_LINE
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3917/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3917/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

That has been valid Fortran since f95, but lfortran disallows it. Perhaps fixing the #3816 bug will fix this one, but #3816 disallowed an intrinsic function in a constant expression, not a non-intrinsic function in a specification expression. Only the offending program and its stderr output are copied here because lfortran's message to stdout was very long. The program:
```
! program specificationexpr.f90
module specificationmod
contains
  pure integer function nlen(n) ! number of decimal digits in abs(n)
    integer,intent(in):: n
    nlen = int(log10(real(abs(n),kind(1d0))+0.1d0))+1
  end function nlen
end module specificationmod

program specificationexpr
  use  specificationmod, only: nlen
  character(22):: cinput='42 666 -42 -666 10 9 0'
  integer     :: input(7),i
  read(cinput,*) input
  do i = 1,7
     print *,'input was "'//i0(input(i))//'"'
  end do
contains
  pure function i0(n) result(outstring)
    integer,intent(in):: n
    character(nlen(n)+1):: outstring ! spec.expr with nlen; +1 in case n<0
    write(outstring,"(I0)") n
  end function i0
end program specificationexpr
```
The lfortran stderr output:
```
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ lfortran specificationexpr.f90 > speccompile
code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Call parameter type does not match function signature!
  %input = alloca [7 x i32], align 4
 i32*  call void @_lfortran_string_read(i8* %4, i8* getelementptr inbounds ([3 x i8], [3 x i8]* @5, i32 0, i32 0), [7 x i32]* %input)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3925/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3925/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

```
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ cat testentry2.f90
! Is entry OK? Must be in an external or module subprogram
module entrymod
  implicit none
contains
  function firstname(n)
    integer,intent(in)::n
    character(24):: firstname, entryname
    write(firstname,"(A,I0)") 'via function name: ',n
    return
    entry entryname(n)
    write(entryname,"(A,I0)") 'via entry: ',n
  end function firstname
end module entrymod

program testentry2
  use entrymod
  implicit none
  print *, entryname(42)
  print *, firstname(666)
end program

(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ lfortran testentry2.f90
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
```
But this one compiles and runs correctly:
```
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ cat testentry.f90
! Is entry OK? Must be in an external or module subprogram
  implicit none
  call indirect
  call direct
end program

subroutine direct
  print *,'entering at direct'
  return
  entry indirect
  print *,'entering at indirect'
end subroutine direct
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ lfortran testentry.f90
entering at indirect
entering at direct
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ 
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3935/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3935/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Or is the trouble with the ```exit``` statement? Evidence:
```
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ cat testio.f90
program testio
  implicit none
  real x
  integer io
  character(3):: data = 'bad'
  do
     read(data,*,iostat=io) x
     if (io/=0) exit ! out of loop
     print *,'Loop should have exited!'
     exit
  end do
  print *,'Program ending.'
end program testio
(lf2) john:~/Test/Glist$ lfortran testio.f90 > testio.out
code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Call parameter type does not match function signature!
  %x = alloca float, align 4
 i32*  call void @_lfortran_string_read(i8* %4, i8* getelementptr inbounds ([3 x i8], [3 x i8]* @1, i32 0, i32 0), float* %x)
```
The ```testio.out``` file was
```
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@testio.data = internal global i8* null
@0 = private unnamed_addr constant [4 x i8] c"bad\00", align 1

  br i1 %6, label %then, label %else

then:                                             ; preds = %loop.body
  br label %loop.end

unreachable_after_exit:                           ; No predecessors!
  br label %ifcont

else:                                             ; preds = %loop.body

ifcont:                                           ; preds = %else, %unreachable_after_exit

other compiler I tried allowed the variable after ```iostat=``` in a ```read``` statement to be an element of an integer array but lfortran said it was not an integer. #3936 also involved ```iostat=``` but no array, and the error messages from lfortran are quite different. The program
```
 program iostatarray
  integer, parameter:: sp = kind(1e0), dp = kind(1d0)
  integer ios(2)
  real(sp) xsp
  real(dp) xdp
  character(3):: cx = '666'
  read(cx,*,iostat=ios(1)) xsp
  if(ios(1)/=0)print *, 'Error in read(cx,*,iostat=ios(1)) xsp'
  print *,'xsp =',xsp
  read(cx,*,iostat=ios(2)) xdp
  if(ios(2)/=0)print *, 'Error in read(cx,*,iostat=ios(2)) xdp'
  print *,'xdp =',xdp
end program iostatarray
```
gave this output in my Ubuntu 22.04 Linux system:
```
semantic error: `iostat` must be of type, Integer
 --> iostatarray.f90:7:3
  |
7 |   read(cx,*,iostat=ios(1)) xsp
  |   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
